title: The Macro Minute: Did the US government just rubberstamp the AI bubble?

Happy Monday out there team 42, it's your skipper here, Darius Dale, to present our macro minute for Monday, June 15th, 2026. As always, we'll start with the executive summary from today's lead off morning note, so let's dive right in. Today's key macro question is did the US government just rubber stamp the AI bubble? The short answer is yes. National security priorities are increasingly governing US frontier model deployment because these models are now viewed as strategic assets subject to tight access controls. This will only enhance investor confidence in the duration and ex-post returns of the AI capex bubble and the 42 macro paradigm seat belt market that is supporting it. In terms of analytical nuance, Washington's striking decision to block foreign use of Anthropic's BP top models highlights the Trump administration's fresh appetite for regulating this critical sector while signaling to Silicon Valley that the technology remains poorly understood and unpredictable in its consequences. Officials issued an unprecedented directive requiring the startups to cut off its most advanced platforms for every foreign national acting after learning that the recently launched Fable 5 model safeguards could be circumvented prompting alarm over what the company deemed a disproportionate reaction that might, quote, "halt all new model developments for all frontier model providers." Despite a June executive order pledging not to mandate Silicon Valley's cooperation, the administration now intends to wield exceptional authority to direct AI builders on security matters that earlier order disavowed any AI licensing scheme, yet officials have effectively established a new one anyway. This comes amid companies increasingly turning to the stock market to raise capital because of the relative attractiveness of equity financing. A wave of fresh equity issuance is set to hit the US stock market around 1.5 trillion over the coming 2 years. That marks a potentially dramatic reversal from the 12 trillion in equity supply that S&P 500 buybacks alone eliminated across two decades. Roughly 160 firms have unveiled plans to raise more than a 120 billion in IPV IPOs year-to-date, topping the prior 2 years combined. Counting deals from public firms, too, fresh supply equity supply tops at $360 billion, marking the strongest first half showing in half a decade. So, they're getting up. So, no, I think the key takeaway here is that, you know, if if if investors get a lot of confidence that the government is going to, you know, effectively, you know, pseudo nationalize these companies because they're so powerful and they must be nationalized to some degree, that ultimately means that, you know, the government is essentially rubber stamping the effectiveness of the tools and then it's going to give investors in the private sector confidence in those tools and the eventual deployment and the productivity enhancing benefits. All right, if we can we can create >> [laughter] >> some sort of, you know, weapon of mass destruction with the technology, surely you can enhance the productivity of doing your white-collar job. So, that's that's that was my key takeaway from that. Transitioning to the 42 macro dashboard, as always, we wrap up with a question from our community. This one's titled initial claims rate of change. Says, "Dear Darios, thanks for all the insightful work. Just wanted to quickly check in your labor market indicator checklist of the chart with the rate of change for initial claims among other metrics. We are seeing an uptick in claims, but how distant are we from your pain threshold?" Uh so, the question so, key takeaway is that if you look at the most recent four-week moving average of initial claims, the rate of change of that, the three-month annualized rate of change of that, we are now approaching a threshold that has historically been consistent with the start of recessions. The median value that we've observed at the start of recessions, um continuing claims are way off their respective their recession signaling threshold, as are four of the five 42 macro fat five recession signaling indicators. In fact, of of those five, only one, the University of Michigan employment survey, is breaching its quote-unquote recession signaling threshold, which again is the median value of the three-month annualized rate change of that particular time series observed at the start of recessions. And so, you know, this is an economy that continues to be growing well above trend on both a on a nominal basis where you know, we're kind of accelerating at trend on a real basis and ultimately we think that that that acceleration is going to extend itself in the coming quarters. And so, you know, we continue to see paradigm C as a booming US economy growing at about 100 basis points above trend on a real basis close to about 200 you know, even 300 basis points above trend on a nominal basis throughout 2026 and 2027. That in our opinion continues to represent meaningful upside to earnings estimates as well. So, we'll wrap up there. Darius Dale here presenting our macro minute for Monday, June 15th, 2026. Best of luck out there today. We'll catch back here tomorrow. Cheers.
